coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets by pyrolysis of novel metal–organic framework compounds
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Qiliang
Zhang, Zhaochun
Yu, Zhenwei
coredata.dc:description: Here, the carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets with novel superstructures are successfully synthesized simultaneously from a hydrothermal preparation and thermal decomposition of a porous precursor of metal–organic frameworks. Porous metal–organic frameworks are prepared by the hydrothermal process by using zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid and 4,4′-bipyridine as the starting materials. Fluorescence spectrophotometer, X-ray powder diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy were used to characterize the structure and property. The results show the coexistence of carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets. The carbon quantum dots size is about 4nm. Particularly, zinc oxide nanosheets show a new triangular sheet structure that has almost the same size. Strong ultraviolet emission of this coexistence system should be useful in developing visible light-emitting and nanophotonic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc oxide nanosheets
Metal–organic frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The thermokinetic analysis of the MOFs
Structural and morphological characteristics of CQDs and ZnO nanosheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The flow chart of the experiment.
(a) Drawing of the asymmetric unit. (b) Three-dimensional framework of the MOFs.
(a) TG and DTG curves of MOFs. (b) Y–X curves at the first and second zone of MOFs for the Freeman-Carroll’s method.

XRD pattern of gray powders. The broad (002) diffraction peaks of graphene and (100), (002), (101), (110), (103) and (112) peaks of ZnO in the XRD patterns provide evidence that carbon dots and zinc oxide are coexist.

(a) HRTEM images of carbon quantum dots. (b) Typical TEM images of carbon quantum dots. (c) Magnified TEM images obtained from the ZnO nanosheets. (d) HRTEM images of the different location of ZnO nanosheets.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-ZnO nanosheets at room temperature, λ ex =300nm. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at room temperature.
Bond lengths [A] and angles [deg] for the compound.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Symmetry transformations used to generate equivalent atoms: #1, x−1/2, −y +1, −z +1/2; #2, −x +1/2, y, z−1/2; #3, −x, −y +1, −z; #4, −x +1/2, −y +2, z; #5, −x +1/2, y, z +1/2; #6 x +1/2, −y +1, −z +1/2.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Kinetic parameters obtained from Freeman-Carroll’s method for the materials.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets by pyrolysis of novel metal–organic framework compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Information Materials, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, the carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets with novel superstructures are successfully synthesized simultaneously from a hydrothermal preparation and thermal decomposition of a porous precursor of metal–organic frameworks. Porous metal–organic frameworks are prepared by the hydrothermal process by using zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid and 4,4′-bipyridine as the starting materials. Fluorescence spectrophotometer, X-ray powder diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy were used to characterize the structure and property. The results show the coexistence of carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets. The carbon quantum dots size is about 4nm. Particularly, zinc oxide nanosheets show a new triangular sheet structure that has almost the same size. Strong ultraviolet emission of this coexistence system should be useful in developing visible light-emitting and nanophotonic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their unique optical and electronic properties caused by the quantum confinement and edge effects, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted considerable interest for the promising candidates to replace the conventional phosphor materials [1–7]. Unlike the traditional semiconductor QDs based on metallic elements, such as CdS, CdSe, PbSe and Ag2S that more or less suffer from the problems of toxicity, hydrophobicity and high cost, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have great superiority of low toxicity, stable fluorescent performance, easy to be chemically modified, good compatibility with organic, inorganic and biological molecules [8,9]. Therefore, CQDs have been widely used in drug analysis, bio-labeling and medical field. Refer to the synthesis routes of CQDs, a variety of synthesis approaches including pyrolysis [10], electrochemical exfoliation [6,11–13], incomplete combustion oxidation [14], acidic oxidation [15], laser ablation [16], hydrothermal treatments [17], microwave/ultrasonic passivation [7,18], and plasma treatment [19] in recent years have been developed to prepare CQDs with various precursors (such as graphite oxide [20], citric acid [21], glycerol [22], coffee grounds [23], soy milk [24], grass [25], and egg [19]).

We selected the metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) that had not been reported as precursor of pyrolysis. MOF [26–28] is a kind of porous material, in which inorganic assemblies are joined by multitopic organic ligand. Because of the special structure of MOFs, we not only obtain CQDs, but also found that ZnO nanosheets. We know various ZnO nanostructures, including wires, belts, cables and tubes, combs, tetrapods, rings and disks, tower-like, rotor-like, flower-like [29,30], oriented helical nanorod arrays [31] and other complex nanostructures [32]. But, characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the ZnO nanosheets we prepared shows a new triangular sheet structure. Besides, CQDs and ZnO nanosheets formed a coexistence system. The study we using fluorospectrophotometer reveal coexistence system has strong ultraviolet emission. In general, not only CQDs, but also nano-ZnO has great superiority of low toxicity, stable fluorescent performance. Therefore, the coexistence system not only can be used to make biological fluorescence probe, but also have potential in developing visible light-emitting and nanophotonic devices.

Firstly, 0.3mol of 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid (OBA), 0.3mol of zinc nitrate hexahydrate, and 0.3mol of 4,4′-bipyridine were dissolved in a mixed solution of 10mL of N,N-dimethyl-formamide and 5mL of ethanol. The mixed solution was further magnetically stirred for 1h, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 50mL capacity. Put the autoclave at 359K for 24h. After being cooled to room temperature, white crystalline powder we obtained was washed several times with ethanol to remove the impurities and then dried at 333K for 1h. The crystal data of the white crystalline powder were collected by single crystal X-ray diffraction (SMART APEX II, BRUKER AXS GMBH) with ω =2θ at room temperature. The program of SAINT-5.0 and SHELXTL-97 were used for data reduction and structure analysis, respectively. Thermal analysis that in air atmosphere take heating rate of 15K/min. The instrument is from NETZSCH Grinding & Dispersing.

Subsequently, put obtained white crystalline powder at 663K for 2h in a temperature-programmed Muffle furnace. After being cooled to room temperature, gray powders we obtained was characterized by XRD (D/MAX-RC model diffractometer) with the use of Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15418nm). We first put the resulting gray powders into ethanol to ultrasound for 2h, and then settling for twenty-four hour. The resulting supernatant is centrifuged and the precipitate removed. For transmission electron microscopy imaging of the structures, a drop of the resulting supernatant was put in a carbon-coated copper grid and dried in atmosphere. The several TEM images of samples were taken using a JEOLJEM-2100F microscope at the accelerating voltage of 200kV. The supernatant were characterized for optical properties through a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and Leica DMIRB analysis was conducted on U-LH100HG. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

Single crystal X-ray analysis revealed the following unit cell parameters. a =16.77746(18)Å, b =11.0642(12)Å, c =22.440(2)Å, α =90°, β =90°, γ =90°, V =4164.8(8)Å3, Z =8. And the crystal system is orthorhombic, the space group is Pcca. Selected bond lengths and angles of the crystal are shown in Table 1 .

The structure of the asymmetric unit is shown in Fig. 2 a. In the asymmetric unit of compounds, including a six metal zinc coordination center, a 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid, a rigid 4,4′-bipyridine and an object of water molecules. In the structure, Zn1 form coordination bonds with the O1 of the carboxyl group and the N1 of 4,4′-bipyridine. Their bond lengths are 2.014(4)Å, 2.032(4)Å. The bond angle of N1–Zn1–O1 is 101.68(16)°. Fig. 2b shows a three-dimensional structure of the compound. Observing from the image of Fig. 2b, four oxygen atoms that coordinating to the zinc ion comes from different 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid. In the square pyramidal configuration of ZnNO4, the nitrogen atom residing at axial vertices of square pyramidal, while four carboxyl oxygen atoms in the horizontal position. This structure not only containing organic molecules that capable of preparing carbon point, and can obtain ZnO by pyrolysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thermokinetic analysis of the MOFs
ce:section.ce:para:
The study of the MOFs is relevant to pyrolysis because decomposition these ligands occurs at different temperatures. Fig. 3 a is the combined thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and derivative TG (DTG) profile showing the thermal degradation characteristics of MOFs at a heating rate of 15°Cmin− 1. The pyrolysis process can be divided into two main regions: moisture and very light volatiles components removal (<250°C); degradation of 4,4′-bipyridine (360–430°C) and 4,4′-oxybisbenzoic acid decomposition (430–510°C). Much of the devolatilization occurred in the second region of weight loss, a result of the thermal breaking of weak bonds in the porous structure of the constituent components of the MOFs and the formation of stronger, more stable bonds to take their place. Fig. 3b shows the Y–X curves at the first and second zone of the second region for the Freeman-Carroll’s method. According to nonisothermal kinetic theory, the thermal decomposition kinetic equation of solid-state material follows:
(1)
d α
dT
=
A
β
exp
-
E
RT
( 1 - α )
n
where β is the constant heating rate (K/min), E is the activation energy (kJ/mol), A is the pre-exponential factor (s− 1), T is the absolute temperature (K), R is the universal gas constant (8.314Jmol− 1 K− 1), and (1−α) n is a reaction model function which has various forms depending on the physical mechanism. α is calculated from the TG curve:
(2)
α = (
m
0
- m ) / (
m
0
-
m
∞
)
where m 0, m, and m∞ are the initial, instantaneous, and final sample masses, respectively.

According to the Freeman-Carroll’s method, we get
(3)
Δ log
d α
dT
Δ log ( 1 - α )
= n -
E
2.3 R
·
Δ
1
T
Δ log ( 1 - α )

Setting
Y =
Δ
log

dT
Δ log ( 1 - α )
and
X =
Δ
1
T
Δ log ( 1 - α )
, then Eq. (3) can be put in the form
(4)
Y = n -
E
2.3 R
· X

The activation energy data, pre-exponential factor and order of reaction calculated from the Freeman-Carroll’s method were collected in Table 2 . The activation energy of process ② is higher than that of process ①, while the pre-exponential factor of process ② is smaller than that of process ①. The higher activation energy needs higher temperature, but also more sensitive to changes in temperature. Small pre-exponential factor shows that the number of low activity. From the thermokinetic analysis of the porous materials, it can be read that the thermal degradation of the materials was a complex process with multiple stages. It is crucial to select the appropriate pyrolysis temperature. Therefore, after several attempts, we choose 663.15K as the pyrolysis temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of CQDs and ZnO nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in X-ray diffraction patterns of gray powders (Fig. 4 ), simultaneous observation of Bragg peaks of carbon dots and zinc oxide indicates that the carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets may were successfully synthesized by one-step. The existence of ZnO nanosheets is confirmed by the appearance of strong intensity peaks of (100), (002), (101), (110) and (112) centered at around 2θ =32.09°, 34.5°, 36.48°, 57.0° and 68.14°, respectively and is in good agreement with the JCPDS (no. 44-0058) data. For carbon, the XRD pattern shows a broad peak at 20.1° corresponding to the (002) peak. This indicates the interlayer spacing (d) of the carbon dots (0.44nm) is higher than that of the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33nm) along with the broadness character. A new weak and broad peak appears around 2θ =11.8°, which shows that the crystal structure of graphite is reduced, disturbed somewhat by oxidation, and the interlayer spacing is expanded [33]. The calculated crystallites sizes (derived from Scherrer’s formula) are about 2.6 and 13.7nm for the CQDs and ZnO nanosheets, respectively.

Considering the details of morphology and structure of the as-prepared quantum dots, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy is employed. The formation of CQDs with an average size of 4nm was confirmed from HRTEM micrographs as shown in Fig. 5 a. It clearly reveals that the interplanar spacing is 0.22nm, indicating preferential growth along the (002) direction. Fig. 5b shows the TEM image of the collected CQDs, it is found that they are monodispersed and also that average particle size of the CQDs is in the range of 2–5nm, which are very similar to the one-step ultrasonic synthesized CQDs (2–3nm) [7]. These finer particles with a narrow size distribution are plausible due to the use of milder conditions compared to literature reports. A further magnified image is shown in Fig. 5c, which detects lots of triangular sheets with smaller dimension of approximate 15nm. It is surprising that the ZnO nanosheets building block is clear and in order. Fig. 5d clearly shows that the structure of the ZnO nanosheets looks like a laminar triangle. The enlarged view of the HRTEM image reveals that the interplanar spacing is 0.26nm, which corresponds to the distance between two planes of the hexagonal ZnO phase, indicating preferential growth along the (002) direction. The analysis results further indicate that carbon quantum dots and ZnO nanosheets with novel laminar triangle are successfully synthesized by one-step.

Sample 1 was obtained after a deposition, while sample 2 was obtained after high-speed centrifugation. We believe that, after high speed centrifugation, ZnO nanosheets and CQDs were separated. That is to say, no ZnO nanosheets in sample 2. Fluorescence spectra of two samples were as shown in Fig. 6 .

As shown in Fig. 6a, the plot displays an intense UV emission at about 390nm and a relatively low violet light emission around 420nm. The peak can be well resolved with four Gaussian subpeaks centered at 337, 373, 427 and 500nm. In Fig. 6b, the plot displays an intense UV emission at about 380nm. The peak can be well resolved with four Gaussian subpeaks centered at 363, 414 and 500nm. A comparison of the two fluorescence spectra, we can see that, there are three similar peak position (370, 420 and 500nm). Therefore, the three peaks are attributed to the fluorescence emission peak of CQDs. The intensities of the two peaks at 337 and 500nm are negligible. At the same time, the peak at 420nm is obviously higher in Fig. 6a. We conclude that ZnO nanosheets also exist fluorescence-emission peak at 420nm.

The ultraviolet light emissions centered at 420nm are thought to the recombination of an electron at the zinc interstitial and a hole in the valance band. Moreover, the strong UV emission shows that the concentration of excitons from the recombination of electrons and holes is high. If the recombination of electrons and holes can be decreased effectively, these ZnO nanosheets may be an effective photocatalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants in water [34].

In conclusion, X-ray diffraction and HRTEM image indicate we have prepared the carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide nanosheets via a high temperature pyrolytic cracking. The key to success is firstly select MOFs as precursor. This route offers a simple and fast approach for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots and ZnO nanosheets, which is potential for large-scale production. Moreover, ZnO nanosheets show a new triangular sheet structure. This coexistence system of CQDs and ZnO nanosheets should be useful in developing visible light-emitting and nanophotonic devices. Further efforts are currently being undertaken.
